close_reason: PR #1 reviewed (round 2) and merged. All 3 issues fixed: §4.2 deferred, DiffOp typed patches, ZoneDefinitionProto typed policies.
description: Review PR https://github.com/Tzeusy/tze-hud/pull/1 thoroughly. Original implementation bead: rig-5vq.1. Leave PR comments on notable issues and resolve each thread with a verdict.
notes:
Review target bead: rig-5vq.1. PR: https://github.com/Tzeusy/tze-hud/pull/1
Review complete. 3 issues found and posted as review threads. Merge deferred: mergeStateStatus=CLEAN but UNRESOLVED_COUNT=4 (1 pre-existing gemini thread + 3 new). Issues: (1) §4.2 incremental diff contradicts v1.md deferral of resumable state sync (https://github.com/Tzeusy/tze-hud/pull/1#discussion_r2970891239); (2) DiffOp uses JSON strings for modified fields — anti-pattern on state-stream path (https://github.com/Tzeusy/tze-hud/pull/1#discussion_r2970892219); (3) ZoneDefinitionProto embeds GeometryPolicy/RenderingPolicy as JSON strings (https://github.com/Tzeusy/tze-hud/pull/1#discussion_r2970893088). Also replied to pre-existing gemini cross-reference thread (won't fix this revision). PR needs changes before merge.
Review round 1 complete: 3 issues found (v1.md scope contradiction on diffs, 2x JSON-in-protobuf anti-pattern). Changes requested.
Re-review round 2: fixes pushed for v1.md contradiction, JSON-in-protobuf issues.
title: Conduct a thorough code review of https://github.com/Tzeusy/tze-hud/pull/1

description:
Write the foundational contract RFCs that define every critical component primitive and concept in tze_hud before any code is written. These RFCs translate the heart-and-soul/ doctrine into quantitative, implementable specifications.

## Motivation

heart-and-soul/ establishes *what* and *why*. These RFCs establish *how* — with concrete protocols, data structures, algorithms, performance budgets, platform targets, and success criteria. Each RFC must explicitly reference the doctrine it implements and provide quantitative requirements (not aspirations).

## Deliverables

1. **Scene Contract RFC** — IDs, namespaces, tile/layout algebra, transactions, snapshots, diffs, hit-testing, durable vs ephemeral state. Grounded in: presence.md (tabs, tiles, zones, scene mutations), architecture.md (compositing model, node types, session model), security.md (agent isolation, leases).

2. **Runtime Kernel RFC** — Process boundaries, thread model, frame stages, admission control, budget enforcement, global degradation policy. Grounded in: architecture.md (screen sovereignty, compositor), validation.md (performance budgets, telemetry), failure.md (degradation ladder), security.md (resource governance).

3. **Timing RFC** — Clock domains, sync groups, timestamp semantics, drift rules, deadline behavior. Grounded in: architecture.md (time as first-class API concept, message classes), presence.md (sync-group membership), validation.md (split latency budgets).

4. **Input RFC** — Focus, capture, gesture model, IME, accessibility hooks, local feedback contract, interactive node primitives. Grounded in: presence.md (interaction model, focus, input routing, gesture arbitration, IME/a11y), architecture.md (overlay click-through), v1.md (hit_region node, local-first feedback).

5. **Privacy + Attention Doctrine** — Short addition to heart-and-soul/ crystallizing the attention governance philosophy. Grounded in: privacy.md (viewer context, content classification, interruption classes, quiet hours).

6. **Reference Vertical Slice** — Headless scene graph → one native window → one resident gRPC agent → lease acquisition → one interactive button/hit-region node → telemetry + artifact generation. Proves the RFC contracts are honest and implementable.

## Success Criteria

- Each RFC contains quantitative requirements with units and percentiles
- Each RFC explicitly cites heart-and-soul/ sections it implements
- Each RFC specifies protocols, wire formats, and platform targets
- The vertical slice compiles, runs headlessly, and emits structured telemetry
- All RFCs include Excalidraw diagrams for key concepts
- Agent workers use /excalidraw-diagram to generate documentation

## Technology Targets

- Rust (tokio, tonic, wgpu, winit)
- gRPC with protobuf (tonic)
- Platforms: Linux (X11/Wayland), Windows (Win32/D3D12), macOS (Cocoa/Metal)
- Headless CI: mesa llvmpipe (Linux), WARP (Windows), Metal (macOS)
title: Contract RFCs for tze_hud core primitives

close_reason: PR #1 merged after PR review round 2. Scene Contract RFC 0001 landed on main.
description:
Write the Scene Contract RFC — the foundational data model specification for tze_hud. This RFC defines the scene graph as a pure data structure (no GPU dependency) and specifies every operation that agents and the runtime can perform on it.

## Doctrine References (heart-and-soul/)

- **presence.md §Tabs and tiles** — Tabs are environment modes; tiles are territories with geometry, z-order, update policy, sync-group membership, input affordances, latency class, ownership/lease, resource budget. Nodes live inside tiles.
- **presence.md §Scene mutations are atomic** — Staged + committed atomically per-frame, all-or-nothing. No partial application. No intermediate garbage.
- **presence.md §Zones** — Named pre-defined publishing surfaces with geometry policy, accepted media types, rendering policy, contention policy (latest-wins, stack, merge-by-key, replace).
- **architecture.md §Compositing model** — Three-layer stack (background, content, chrome). Tiles are z-order unit. Intra-tile nodes composited in tree order.
- **architecture.md §Node type extensibility** — Registered, not hardcoded. Each declares resource requirements, serialization, telemetry surface, input behavior. V1: solid_color, text_markdown, static_image, hit_region.
- **architecture.md §Session model** — One bidirectional gRPC stream per agent. Scene mutations multiplexed over it.
- **architecture.md §Resource lifecycle** — Reference-counted, owned by scene graph. Deterministic deallocation.
- **security.md §Agent isolation** — No cross-agent content access. Topology visible, content opaque.
- **security.md §Capability scopes** — Additive, granular, revocable. Per-session.
- **validation.md §DR-V1** — Scene model separable from renderer. Pure data structure.
- **v1.md §Scene model** — Tabs CRUD, tiles CRUD, 4 node types, sync groups, atomic batch mutations, zone system.

## Required Sections

### 1. Identity Model
- Scene-global UUID scheme for tabs, tiles, nodes, zones, leases, agents
- Namespace isolation: agent → namespace mapping, namespace → tile ownership
- ID generation: UUIDv7 (time-ordered) for scene objects, content-addressed hash for immutable resources
- Cross-reference rules: which IDs can appear in which contexts

### 2. Scene Graph Structure
- Tab → Tile → Node hierarchy with formal tree invariants
- Tile properties: bounds (Rect), z_order (u32), opacity (f32), input_mode (enum), sync_group (Option<SyncGroupId>), lease_id, resource_budget
- Node types: trait definition, V1 concrete types with full field specs
- Zone registry: zone definition schema, zone-to-tile mapping, contention policies
- Invariants: no overlapping exclusive tiles in same z-order, all tiles within tab bounds, node tree is acyclic

### 3. Transaction Model
- Mutation batch format: Vec<SceneMutation> enum (CreateTile, DeleteTile, UpdateTile, CreateNode, ..., PublishToZone, ...)
- Staging → validation → commit pipeline
- Validation rules per mutation type (lease check, budget check, bounds check, type check)
- Rejection semantics: entire batch rejected on any validation failure, structured error per failed mutation
- Commit semantics: applied atomically in one frame, monotonic sequence number
- Concurrency: multiple agents' batches queued, applied in arrival order within same frame

### 4. Snapshots and Diffs
- Full scene snapshot format (serializable, deterministic)
- Incremental diff format for efficient state sync on reconnect
- Snapshot triggers: agent reconnect, debug dump, telemetry emission
- Diff computation: O(changed_nodes) not O(total_nodes)

### 5. Hit-Testing
- Spatial query: point-in-tile, point-in-node within tile
- Z-order traversal: chrome layer first, then content tiles highest-z first, then nodes in reverse tree order
- Hit-test result: (tile_id, node_id, local_coords) or passthrough
- Overlay mode: passthrough for non-interactive regions (per architecture.md §Window model)

### 6. Durable vs Ephemeral State
- Durable: agent registrations, capability grants, tab/layout config, user preferences (survive restart)
- Ephemeral: scene graph state, active leases, live content (lost on restart)
- Persistence format for durable state (file-based, human-readable per architecture.md §Configuration model)

### 7. Protobuf Schema
- scene.proto with all message types
- Mutation batch as repeated oneof
- Snapshot and diff messages
- Zone registry messages

## Quantitative Requirements

- Snapshot serialization: < 1ms for 100-tile scene on reference hardware
- Diff computation: < 500μs for typical frame delta (1-5 mutations)
- Hit-test: < 100μs for single point query on 50-tile scene
- Transaction validation: < 200μs per batch of 10 mutations
- Scene graph memory: < 1KB per tile + node overhead (excluding texture data)
- Maximum scene size: 256 tabs, 1024 tiles per tab, 64 nodes per tile

## Diagrams Required

Use /excalidraw-diagram to create:
1. Scene graph hierarchy (Tab → Tile → Node tree with zone overlay)
2. Transaction pipeline (stage → validate → commit → present)
3. Hit-test traversal order (chrome → z-ordered tiles → nodes)
4. ID namespace isolation model

## Platform Targets

- Pure Rust, no platform-specific code (scene graph is platform-independent)
- Serialization: protobuf (prost) for wire, serde for config/debug
- All operations must work in #[no_std] compatible subset where possible
notes:
PR review requested changes: (1) §4.2 reconnect diff system contradicts v1.md which defers resumable state sync, (2) DiffOp uses raw JSON strings in protobuf violating no-JSON-on-hot-paths, (3) ZoneDefinitionProto uses JSON strings for policy instead of typed proto messages.
Fixes pushed for all 3 review issues. Awaiting re-review.
title: Write Scene Contract RFC: IDs, namespaces, tile algebra, transactions, diffs, hit-testing

description:
Write the Runtime Kernel RFC — the execution model specification for the tze_hud compositor process. This RFC defines how the runtime is structured as an OS process, how work is scheduled across threads, how frames are produced, and how resource budgets are enforced.

## Doctrine References (heart-and-soul/)

- **architecture.md §The screen is sovereign** — Runtime owns pixels, timing, composition, input routing, permissions, resource budgets, arbitration, safety, fallback. Rule 1: LLMs never sit in the frame loop.
- **architecture.md §Compositing model** — Three-layer stack (background, content, chrome). Compositor manages layered surface stack.
- **architecture.md §Resource lifecycle** — Reference-counted, scene-graph-owned. Deterministic deallocation. Resource leaks are correctness bugs.
- **architecture.md §Versioning** — gRPC protocol versioned. Agents declare version on handshake.
- **security.md §Resource governance** — Per-session enforced limits: texture memory, bandwidth, concurrent streams, CPU time, active leases. Warning → throttle → revoke escalation.
- **failure.md §Core principle** — Runtime always usable even when agents fail. Worst case: "some tiles are empty."
- **failure.md §Degradation axes** — Ordered: coalesce → reduce media quality → reduce streams → simplify rendering → shed tiles → audio-first fallback.
- **validation.md §Performance budgets** — p99 frame_time < 16.6ms. input_to_local_ack p99 < 4ms. input_to_scene_commit p99 < 50ms. input_to_next_present p99 < 33ms.
- **validation.md §DR-V2** — Headless rendering: full frame to offscreen texture, no display server. Feature-equivalent to windowed.
- **validation.md §DR-V3** — Structured telemetry every frame.
- **v1.md §Compositor** — wgpu headless + windowed, tile composition with z-order, alpha blending, 60fps on reference hardware.
- **v1.md §Window modes** — Fullscreen and overlay (HUD). Runtime config, not compile-time.

## Required Sections

### 1. Process Architecture
- Single-process compositor (no child process per agent — agents are external gRPC clients)
- Process entry point: event loop owner (winit), gRPC server (tonic), telemetry emitter
- Headless mode: same process, swapped surface (offscreen texture vs window)
- Graceful shutdown sequence: drain agent sessions → flush telemetry → release GPU resources → exit

### 2. Thread Model
- **Main thread**: winit event loop, input processing, local feedback, frame presentation
- **Compositor thread**: scene graph commit, render command encoding, GPU submission
- **Network thread(s)**: tokio runtime for gRPC server, agent session management, MCP bridge
- **Telemetry thread**: async structured telemetry emission, sampling, aggregation
- Thread communication: bounded channels (crossbeam or tokio::sync::mpsc) with explicit backpressure
- Thread priority: main thread elevated (SCHED_RR or equivalent) for input latency guarantee
- Thread count: fixed at startup, no dynamic thread spawning in steady state

### 3. Frame Pipeline (per-frame stages)
Define the ordered stages within each frame:
1. **Input drain**: collect all pending input events from winit (< 500μs budget)
2. **Local feedback**: update press/hover/focus state locally (< 500μs budget, contributes to 4ms input_to_local_ack)
3. **Mutation intake**: dequeue committed mutation batches from agent sessions (< 1ms budget)
4. **Scene commit**: apply mutations to scene graph, validate, increment sequence (< 1ms budget)
5. **Layout resolve**: recompute tile bounds, zone geometry, constraints (< 1ms budget)
6. **Render encode**: walk scene graph, encode GPU commands (< 4ms budget)
7. **GPU submit + present**: submit command buffer, present to surface (< 8ms budget)
8. **Telemetry emit**: record per-frame metrics (< 200μs budget, async)
- Total budget: < 16.6ms at 60fps. Stages are profiled individually per validation.md §Layer 3.
- Pipeline must be pipelineable: GPU submit for frame N can overlap with input drain for frame N+1.

### 4. Admission Control
- Agent connection: authenticate → capability negotiation → session stream established
- Maximum concurrent agents: configurable, default 16 resident + 64 guest
- Per-agent resource envelope: {max_tiles: u32, max_texture_bytes: u64, max_update_rate_hz: f32, max_nodes_per_tile: u32}
- Admission rejection: structured error with available capacity, retry-after hint
- Hot-connect: new agents join mid-scene without frame skip

### 5. Budget Enforcement
- Per-agent, per-frame resource tracking: texture memory, mutation count, bandwidth
- Enforcement tiers per security.md: warning event → throttle (coalesce more aggressively, reduce quality) → lease revocation → session termination
- Grace periods: warning=5s, throttle=30s, revocation=immediate on critical budget violation
- Global budget: total texture memory, total active tiles, total GPU command buffer size
- Frame-time guardian: if frame N exceeds 16.6ms, shed lowest-priority work in frame N+1

### 6. Degradation Policy
- Trigger: frame_time_p95 > 14ms (85% of budget) over 10-frame window
- Degradation ladder (ordered, per failure.md):
  1. Coalesce state-stream updates to 2Hz (from default agent rate)
  2. Reduce texture quality for non-foreground tiles (half resolution)
  3. Disable transparency blending (opaque compositing only)
  4. Shed tiles below priority threshold
  5. Emergency: render only chrome layer + highest-priority tile
- Recovery: hysteresis — must sustain frame_time_p95 < 12ms for 30 frames before stepping back up
- All transitions logged in telemetry with structured reason

### 7. Window Surface Management
- Fullscreen: winit fullscreen window, opaque background, all input captured
- Overlay/HUD: transparent borderless always-on-top, selective click-through
- Platform-specific click-through: WM_NCHITTEST (Windows), hitTest (macOS), XShape (X11), wlr-layer-shell (Wayland)
- Mode switch: runtime reconfiguration without process restart
- Multi-monitor: v1 targets single monitor; multi-monitor is documented as future extension

### 8. Headless Mode
- Same frame pipeline, GPU submit targets offscreen texture
- wgpu backend: mesa llvmpipe (Linux), WARP (Windows), Metal (macOS)
- Pixel readback for test assertions (Layer 1 validation)
- No input events from OS — synthetic injection only
- Feature flag: --features headless or runtime config

## Quantitative Requirements

- Frame pipeline total: p99 < 16.6ms (normalized to hardware calibration vector)
- Input drain + local feedback: p99 < 4ms (input_to_local_ack)
- Agent mutation intake to next present: p99 < 33ms (input_to_next_present)
- Agent connection establishment: < 50ms from TCP connect to session-ready
- Degradation response: within 1 frame of trigger detection
- Memory overhead per agent session: < 64KB (excluding tile content)
- Telemetry emission: < 200μs per frame, non-blocking

## Diagrams Required

Use /excalidraw-diagram to create:
1. Thread model: main, compositor, network, telemetry — with channel topology
2. Frame pipeline: 8 stages with time budgets per stage
3. Degradation ladder: trigger → stages → recovery with hysteresis
4. Process architecture: event loop, gRPC server, GPU, telemetry as blocks

## Platform Targets

- Rust: tokio (network), winit (events/window), wgpu (GPU)
- Thread priority: platform-specific (pthread on Linux/macOS, SetThreadPriority on Windows)
- Headless: mesa llvmpipe, WARP, Metal
- CI: all three platforms, headless mode mandatory
title: Write Runtime Kernel RFC: process model, thread architecture, frame pipeline, budgets

description:
Write the Timing RFC — the temporal semantics specification for tze_hud. This RFC defines how time is represented, propagated, and enforced across the entire system. Time is a first-class API concept per doctrine; this RFC makes that concrete.

## Doctrine References (heart-and-soul/)

- **architecture.md §Time is a first-class API concept** — "Arrival time is not presentation time." Every meaningful realtime payload carries: present_at, effective_after, expires_at, sequence, priority, coalesce_key, sync_group.
- **architecture.md §Message classes** — Four traffic classes with different delivery/timing semantics: transactional (reliable/ordered/acked), state-stream (reliable/ordered/coalesced), ephemeral realtime (low-latency/droppable/latest-wins), clocked media/cues (scheduled against media/display clock).
- **presence.md §Tabs and tiles** — Tiles have sync-group membership and latency class.
- **presence.md §Zones** — Zone contention policies have temporal semantics (latest-wins, stack with timeout, auto-clear).
- **validation.md §Split latency budgets** — input_to_local_ack p99 < 4ms, input_to_scene_commit p99 < 50ms, input_to_next_present p99 < 33ms.
- **validation.md §Chaos testing the timing model** — Inject clock skew, jitter, discontinuities. Sync groups must reconverge. Timed cues must not pile up. Expired leases must be revoked even if clock jumps.
- **failure.md §Agent is slow** — Staleness indicators when data is stale beyond threshold.
- **v1.md §Sync groups** — Basic membership, no media clock yet. V1 defers clocked media/cue message class.

## Required Sections

### 1. Clock Domains
- **Display clock**: monotonic, driven by vsync/frame presentation. Source of truth for "when does content appear on screen." Resolution: display refresh period (16.6ms at 60Hz).
- **System monotonic clock**: std::time::Instant (Rust). Used for internal scheduling, lease TTL, telemetry timestamps. Monotonic guarantee required.
- **Agent-reported timestamps**: untrusted. Agents may report timestamps from their own clocks. The runtime translates agent time to system time via offset estimation on session handshake (NTP-lite: 3 round-trips, median offset).
- **Media clock** (v1 deferred, but designed): per-stream clock driven by media pipeline (GStreamer). Used for AV sync, subtitle alignment, cue timing. Not implemented in v1 but the timestamp format must accommodate it.
- Clock domain registry: each sync group declares its reference clock domain.

### 2. Timestamp Semantics
Define the core timestamp type and its semantic fields:
```
struct TimedPayload<T> {
    data: T,
    present_at: Option<Timestamp>,      // When to show (None = ASAP)
    effective_after: Option<Timestamp>,  // Don't apply before this
    expires_at: Option<Timestamp>,       // Discard after this
    sequence: u64,                       // Monotonic per-source ordering
    priority: Priority,                  // For shedding under load
    coalesce_key: Option<CoalesceKey>,   // For state-stream dedup
    sync_group: Option<SyncGroupId>,     // For synchronized presentation
}
```
- Timestamp type: i64 nanoseconds since epoch (sufficient for ~292 years, compatible with protobuf Timestamp)
- Monotonicity: sequence numbers are per-agent, per-stream, monotonically increasing
- Ordering: within a coalesce_key, only the highest-sequence payload is presented

### 3. Sync Groups
- Definition: a set of tiles/nodes that must present changes atomically in the same frame
- Membership: tile or node declares sync_group_id at creation
- Commit rule: mutations targeting sync group members are held until all members' mutations for that logical update are received, or a deadline expires
- Deadline: configurable per sync group, default 2 frames (33ms at 60Hz). If deadline expires, present what's available + mark incomplete members as stale.
- V1 scope: sync groups coordinate scene-level mutations only (no media clock). Media sync is designed into the data model but not implemented.

### 4. Coalescing Rules
- State-stream payloads with the same coalesce_key: keep only the latest (highest sequence)
- Coalescing window: per-frame. All state-stream updates received between frame N-1 present and frame N commit are coalesced.
- Coalescing is per-agent, per-coalesce_key. Cross-agent coalescing never happens.
- Ephemeral realtime: latest-wins per source. No queue. If two cursor positions arrive in one frame, only the latest is used.
- Transactional: never coalesced. Every transaction is applied in order.

### 5. Drift and Skew Rules
- Agent clock offset: estimated on handshake, re-estimated every 60s via ping-pong
- Maximum allowed offset: 500ms. Agents with larger offset receive a warning. Agents with offset > 2s have their timestamps treated as untrusted (all present_at treated as ASAP).
- Sync group drift: maximum allowed inter-member presentation skew is 1 frame (16.6ms at 60Hz). If exceeded, stale members get a staleness indicator.
- Monotonicity violation: if an agent sends a sequence number <= the last seen, the payload is dropped and a warning event is sent.
- Clock jump detection: if system monotonic clock jumps forward > 1s (e.g., VM resume), all pending expires_at payloads are evaluated immediately. Lease TTLs are adjusted.

### 6. Deadline Behavior
- present_at in the past: present immediately (next frame). Log the lateness in telemetry.
- expires_at in the past: discard silently. Log the discard in telemetry.
- Lease TTL expired: revoke immediately regardless of other timing. Lease TTL uses system monotonic clock, not agent-reported time.
- Sync group deadline exceeded: present available members, mark missing as stale, do not block the frame.
- Quiet hours interaction: timestamps on queued (non-urgent) content are preserved. When quiet hours end, queued content with valid expires_at is presented; expired content is discarded.

### 7. Telemetry Timestamps
- Every telemetry record: frame_number (u64), frame_start_mono (Timestamp), present_time (Timestamp), per-stage durations
- Latency measurements: all three split latencies tracked per-event with source timestamp
- Drift tracking: per-agent clock offset history, per-sync-group skew history
- All timestamps in telemetry are system monotonic (not agent-reported)

### 8. Protobuf Schema
- timing.proto: Timestamp message, TimedPayload wrapper, SyncGroup definition, ClockDomain enum
- Reuse protobuf well-known Timestamp type where compatible, or define custom i64-nanos for precision

## Quantitative Requirements

- Timestamp resolution: 1 nanosecond
- Clock offset estimation accuracy: < 5ms error after handshake
- Sync group deadline: configurable, default 33ms (2 frames at 60Hz)
- Maximum sync group skew: 16.6ms (1 frame at 60Hz)
- Coalescing overhead: < 50μs per frame for 100 pending state-stream updates
- Clock jump detection: within 1 frame of occurrence
- Stale content detection: within 2 frames of threshold breach

## Diagrams Required

Use /excalidraw-diagram to create:
1. Clock domain relationships: display clock, system monotonic, agent clocks, media clock (future)
2. Coalescing pipeline: arrival → coalesce window → frame commit → present
3. Sync group commit/deadline: members arrive → hold → deadline → present/stale
4. Message class timing: transactional vs state-stream vs ephemeral vs clocked

## Platform Targets

- Rust std::time::Instant for monotonic clock (cross-platform)
- Platform vsync: wgpu present_mode (Fifo = vsync, Mailbox = low-latency)
- Protobuf: prost with custom Timestamp codec
- GStreamer clock integration designed but not wired in v1
title: Write Timing RFC: clock domains, sync groups, timestamp semantics, drift, deadlines

description:
Write the Input RFC — the interaction model specification for tze_hud. This RFC defines how user input (pointer, touch, keyboard, accessibility) flows through the system, how local feedback is guaranteed, and how the interactive hit-region node primitive works.

## Doctrine References (heart-and-soul/)

- **presence.md §Interaction** — Two-way interaction mandatory. Supports: touch, pointer, buttons, keyboard/mouse, voice triggers, sensor-initiated interrupts, app-to-agent callbacks. Local-first: pressed state, focus ring, visual ack happen locally and instantly.
- **presence.md §Focus model** — Per-tile, not per-agent. At most one tile has keyboard focus. Runtime manages focus transitions. Focus is local-first: visual indicator updates before agent notification.
- **presence.md §Input routing and bubbling** — Route through scene graph: chrome layer (always wins) → tiles in z-order (highest first) → nodes within tile. Unhandled events bubble: node → tile → tab → runtime.
- **presence.md §Gesture arbitration** — Runtime arbitrates cross-tile gestures. Default: tile where gesture started owns it.
- **presence.md §IME, text input, and accessibility** — First-class, not afterthought. Runtime exposes a11y metadata via platform APIs. Agents declare semantic roles. Runtime bridges to platform.
- **architecture.md §Window model** — Overlay mode: selective click-through. Interactive regions capture input; non-interactive pass through. Per-region determination follows scene graph.
- **v1.md §Interaction** — Mouse/pointer, touch, local-first feedback (press states, focus rings, hover highlights). Input events forwarded to owning agent. At least one retained interactive primitive: hit_region node with local pressed/hovered state, focus semantics, agent callback on activation.
- **validation.md §Split latency budgets** — input_to_local_ack p99 < 4ms.
- **failure.md §What the user always sees** — Screen responsive to touch/input within 4ms. Tab switching works. Safe mode override works.

## Required Sections

### 1. Input Event Model
- Raw event types from winit: PointerMoved, PointerPressed, PointerReleased, KeyPressed, KeyReleased, TouchStart, TouchMove, TouchEnd, Scroll, IMECommit, IMEPreedit
- Normalized internal event: InputEvent { kind, position (logical coords), timestamp (system mono), modifiers, device_id, touch_id (for multi-touch) }
- Coordinate spaces: physical pixels → logical pixels (DPI-scaled) → scene coords → tile-local coords → node-local coords
- Event timestamps: system monotonic at OS event receipt, not at processing time

### 2. Hit-Testing Pipeline
- Depends on Scene Contract RFC for spatial query definition
- Pipeline: raw position → scene coords → chrome layer hit test → z-ordered tile hit test → node hit test within tile
- Chrome always wins: runtime UI elements always capture input regardless of agent content
- Overlay mode passthrough: non-interactive regions return HitResult::Passthrough, which the window manager translates to click-through (WM_NCHITTEST HTTRANSPARENT / hitTest nil / XShape mask)
- Dynamic hit-test mask: updated every frame based on active tiles with input affordances. Cached as a bitmask for fast lookup.
- Performance: < 100μs per hit test (from Scene Contract), amortized across frame for hover tracking

### 3. Focus Model
- Focus state: FocusState { focused_tile: Option<TileId>, focused_node: Option<NodeId> }
- Focus transitions: pointer click → focus tile under pointer. Tab key → cycle focusable tiles. Agent cannot forcibly steal focus.
- Focus semantics: focused tile receives keyboard events. Non-focused tiles do not. 
- Visual feedback: focus ring rendered by compositor (chrome layer, not agent-controlled). Style: configurable, default 2px accent-color ring.
- Focus events to agents: FocusGained { tile_id, node_id }, FocusLost { tile_id, node_id } — delivered via agent session stream
- Programmatic focus request: agent can request focus for its own tile (granted if policy allows, never if another tile is mid-interaction)

### 4. Local Feedback Contract
- **THE critical latency path.** input_to_local_ack p99 < 4ms.
- Local feedback is rendered by the compositor, not by the agent. The agent is NOT in the feedback loop for:
  - Hover highlight: tile/node gains subtle highlight when pointer enters bounds
  - Press state: hit_region node visually depresses on pointer-down, releases on pointer-up
  - Focus ring: visual ring appears on focused tile/node
  - Scroll feedback: scroll position updates immediately
- These are compositor-side state machines driven by input events. The agent receives events *after* local feedback is already rendered.
- Implementation: local feedback state is part of the scene graph (per-node: hovered: bool, pressed: bool, focused: bool). Updated in the "local feedback" frame stage (stage 2 in Runtime Kernel RFC) with zero network roundtrip.

### 5. Gesture Model
- Gesture recognition: pointer/touch sequences classified into gestures
  - Tap: down + up within 300ms and 10px movement threshold
  - Long-press: down held > 500ms without movement
  - Drag: down + move beyond 10px threshold
  - Scroll: two-finger or scroll wheel
  - Pinch: two-touch with changing distance (multi-touch only)
  - Swipe: fast directional movement (velocity > 500px/s)
- Gesture ownership: the tile/node where the gesture *starts* (first pointer-down) owns the entire gesture sequence
- Cross-tile gesture arbitration: if a drag starts in tile A and moves to tile B, tile A owns it. Tile B does not receive events for that gesture.
- Gesture cancellation: if the owning tile is deleted or its lease is revoked mid-gesture, the gesture is cancelled and remaining events are discarded
- Gesture events to agents: GestureStart, GestureUpdate, GestureEnd, GestureCancelled — with gesture type, position, velocity, accumulated delta

### 6. Interactive Node Primitives (hit_region)
- V1 ships one retained interactive primitive: hit_region
- hit_region node properties:
  - bounds: Rect (within parent tile)
  - cursor: CursorType (pointer, hand, text, crosshair, etc.)
  - focusable: bool
  - a11y_role: AccessibilityRole (button, link, checkbox, slider, etc.)
  - a11y_label: String
  - enabled: bool (disabled regions show dimmed, don't capture input)
  - local_feedback_style: FeedbackStyle (default, custom colors, none)
- hit_region local state (compositor-managed):
  - hovered: bool (pointer within bounds)
  - pressed: bool (pointer down within bounds)
  - focused: bool (has keyboard focus)
- hit_region agent callbacks:
  - Activated (tap/click completed)
  - PressStart, PressEnd
  - HoverEnter, HoverExit
  - FocusGained, FocusLost
  - KeyEvent (when focused)

### 7. IME and Text Input
- IME lifecycle: StartComposition → PreeditUpdate(text, cursor) → CommitText(final) → EndComposition
- Platform IME integration: winit handles OS-level IME. Runtime bridges events to focused tile's agent.
- Preedit rendering: compositor renders preedit text at the focused node's position (agent does not render preedit)
- CJK, emoji, dictation: all handled through platform IME path
- Text input nodes: future extension. V1 hit_region receives key events; full text editing is deferred.

### 8. Accessibility
- Platform APIs: UI Automation (Windows), NSAccessibility (macOS), AT-SPI (Linux)
- Scene graph → a11y tree mapping: each tile → a11y container, each hit_region → a11y element with role + label
- Focus tracking: a11y focus follows compositor focus
- Screen reader narration: tile names, node labels, state changes (pressed, disabled, checked) announced
- Keyboard navigation: Tab cycles focusable elements, Enter/Space activates, Escape dismisses/unfocuses
- Minimum commitment: v1 exposes the a11y tree with correct roles, labels, and focus. Advanced patterns (live regions, custom actions, drag-and-drop a11y) deferred.

### 9. Protobuf Schema
- input.proto: InputEvent, HitResult, GestureEvent, FocusEvent, IMEEvent, AccessibilityNode
- Agent session stream carries: input events for owned tiles, gesture events, focus events, IME events

## Quantitative Requirements

- Hit-test latency: < 100μs per point query (50-tile scene)
- Local feedback latency (input_to_local_ack): p99 < 4ms
- Focus transition: < 1ms from input event to focus ring update
- Gesture classification: < 2ms from qualifying event to gesture event emission
- A11y tree update: < 5ms after scene commit
- IME preedit render: < 1 frame (16.6ms) from OS event to preedit visible
- Hit-test mask update: < 500μs per frame (for overlay click-through)

## Diagrams Required

Use /excalidraw-diagram to create:
1. Input routing pipeline: OS event → winit → local feedback → hit-test → agent event
2. Focus state machine: unfocused → focused (pointer/tab) → focused (agent request) → unfocused (pointer away/tab/revoked)
3. Gesture lifecycle: down → classify → own → update → end/cancel
4. hit_region node state machine: idle → hovered → pressed → activated → idle
5. Overlay click-through: scene graph → hit-test mask → platform pass-through

## Platform Targets

- Input: winit (cross-platform pointer, keyboard, touch, IME)
- A11y: accesskit (Rust, bridges to all three platform APIs)
- Overlay click-through: WM_NCHITTEST (Win), hitTest (macOS), XShape (X11), wlr-layer-shell (Wayland)
- Multi-touch: supported on touch-capable displays and mobile (winit touch events)
title: Write Input RFC: focus, gestures, IME, accessibility, local feedback, hit-region primitives

close_reason: Simple change merged to main (attention governance doctrine)
description:
Add a short, focused "Attention Governance" section to the heart-and-soul/ doctrine that crystallizes the philosophical stance on attention, privacy, and interruption. This is distinct from privacy.md (which already exists and is detailed) — this is a compact, principled distillation that belongs alongside the core thesis.

## Why This Is Needed

privacy.md exists and is thorough — it covers viewer context, content classification, redaction, interruption classes, quiet hours, multi-viewer scenarios, and topology visibility. But its principles are scattered across implementation detail.

What's missing is a compact doctrinal statement that:
1. Declares attention governance as part of the product's *spirit*, not just its feature set
2. Establishes that the system's relationship to human attention is as fundamental as its relationship to screen pixels
3. Defines the anti-patterns of attention abuse (notification spam, cognitive overload, dark patterns for engagement)
4. States the principles that implementation must serve

This belongs in heart-and-soul/ because it's product doctrine, not implementation detail.

## Doctrine References (heart-and-soul/)

- **privacy.md (entire document)** — Viewer context, content classification (public/household/private/sensitive), redaction behavior, interruption classes (silent/gentle/normal/urgent/critical), quiet hours, multi-viewer policy.
- **vision.md §Non-goals** — "Not a notification engine. If the primary experience becomes a stream of notifications, the product has failed."
- **presence.md §Leases** — "This prevents the system from becoming attention spam. The human must always be able to: dismiss, mute, pin, freeze, shrink, revoke."
- **security.md §Human override** — Human is always the ultimate authority. No agent can prevent dismissal, revocation, muting, freezing, or safe mode.
- **failure.md §What the user always sees** — Screen always responsive. Dismiss all / safe mode always available.

## Deliverable

A new file `heart-and-soul/attention.md` (or a substantial new section in an existing doctrine file — discuss with user) containing:

### 1. Core Principle
The system's relationship to human attention is governed, not exploited. tze_hud is not an attention-capture engine. It is a presence engine. Presence means being available when needed and quiet when not. An agent that demands attention without earning it is a failure of governance, not a feature.

### 2. Attention Budget Concept
Formalize the idea that the screen has an "attention budget" — a finite capacity for demanding the viewer's focus. Interruptions deplete this budget. Silent content does not. The runtime should track and enforce an attention budget analogous to resource budgets.

### 3. Anti-Patterns of Attention
- Notification spam: agents using normal/urgent interruption classes to grab attention for low-value content
- Cognitive overload: too many tiles updating simultaneously, creating visual noise
- Engagement dark patterns: agents optimizing for "screen time" rather than user value
- Interruption escalation: agents upgrading interruption class to bypass quiet hours

### 4. Design Principles for Attention
- Ambient > interruptive: prefer content that can be glanced at over content that demands focus
- Quiet by default: new agents should default to silent/gentle interruption classes
- Earned urgency: urgent/critical interruption is for life-safety and time-critical events, not engagement
- Viewer autonomy: the human decides how much attention to give. The system never guilts, nags, or punishes inattention.
- Attention telemetry: the system can track interruption frequency, dismissal rate, and screen time — but only for governance (rate-limiting noisy agents), never for engagement optimization

### 5. Relationship to Existing Doctrine
Map each principle back to existing heart-and-soul/ sections. This section should feel like a natural extension of vision.md's "not a notification engine" and presence.md's "attention spam" prevention, not a new concern.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. New doctrine file or section exists in heart-and-soul/
2. Content is compact (< 150 lines) and principled (not implementation-prescriptive)
3. References existing privacy.md for implementation detail (no duplication)
4. Explicitly states anti-patterns
5. Updates heart-and-soul/README.md reading order to include the new content
6. Does NOT add implementation requirements — those belong in the RFCs
title: Add privacy + attention governance doctrine to heart-and-soul/

description:
Build a minimal reference vertical slice that proves the RFC contracts are honest and implementable. This is the acid test: if this slice feels clean, the rest of the architecture is probably honest. If it's awkward, the RFCs need revision before broader implementation begins.

## Doctrine References (heart-and-soul/)

- **v1.md §V1 must prove** — "An LLM can hold a tile on a screen." "The lease model works." "Performance is real."
- **v1.md §V1 success criteria** — 3 LLM agents simultaneously holding tiles, governed by leases, at 60fps.
- **validation.md §DR-V1 through DR-V6** — Scene separable from renderer, headless rendering, structured telemetry, deterministic test scenes, trivial headless invocation, no physical GPU for CI.
- **validation.md §The LLM development loop** — Steps 2-4 need no human. The slice must be testable by an LLM.
- **architecture.md §Error model** — Every error is structured, machine-readable, and diagnostic.

## What the Slice Must Demonstrate

This is ONE end-to-end path through the entire stack, touching every layer:

### Layer 1: Headless Scene Graph (Pure Data)
- Create a scene graph in memory with no GPU context (per DR-V1)
- One tab, two tiles (one text, one hit_region), one zone (status-bar)
- Verify scene graph invariants via Layer 0 assertions
- Serialize/deserialize a full snapshot
- Compute a diff after a single mutation

### Layer 2: Native Window + Compositor
- Open a single winit window (or headless offscreen texture)
- Initialize wgpu with appropriate backend (Vulkan/Metal/D3D12 or llvmpipe/WARP)
- Render the scene graph: background layer, two tiles in content layer, chrome layer
- Verify rendering via Layer 1 pixel readback (region color assertions)
- Achieve 60fps on reference hardware, verified via Layer 3 telemetry

### Layer 3: Resident gRPC Agent
- Start a tonic gRPC server alongside the compositor
- A test agent (Rust binary) connects, authenticates (pre-shared key), receives capability grants
- Agent opens a bidirectional session stream
- Agent sends a mutation batch: create tile, create hit_region node, set text content
- Mutation is validated, committed, and rendered in the next frame
- Agent receives scene events (tile created confirmation, input events)

### Layer 4: Lease Acquisition
- Agent requests a surface lease with TTL (60s), capability scope, resource budget
- Runtime grants the lease, agent holds it
- Lease renewal: agent renews before TTL expiry
- Lease revocation: runtime revokes (simulated human override), agent receives notification, tile is cleared
- Reconnection: agent disconnects, reconnects within grace period, reclaims lease

### Layer 5: Interactive Hit-Region
- The hit_region node in the agent's tile responds to pointer input
- Local feedback: hover highlight and press state render within 4ms of input (input_to_local_ack)
- Agent callback: activation event delivered to agent via session stream
- The agent receives the activation event and responds with a scene mutation (e.g., changes tile text content)
- End-to-end: pointer click → local press state → agent receives activation → agent sends mutation → text updates on screen

### Layer 6: Telemetry + Artifacts
- Every frame emits structured JSON telemetry per validation.md §Layer 3
- Per-frame: frame_time, per-stage durations, input latencies (all three split metrics), active leases, resource usage
- Per-session: aggregated p50/p95/p99 for all metrics
- Layer 4 developer visibility artifacts: rendered PNG, telemetry JSON, explanation
- All telemetry is machine-readable and parseable by an LLM

## Success Criteria (Quantitative)

| Metric | Requirement | Measurement |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| Frame time p99 | < 16.6ms (normalized) | Layer 3 telemetry |
| input_to_local_ack p99 | < 4ms | Split latency measurement |
| input_to_scene_commit p99 | < 50ms | Split latency measurement |
| input_to_next_present p99 | < 33ms | Split latency measurement |
| Scene graph ops | < 100μs hit-test, < 200μs validation, < 500μs diff | Layer 0 benchmarks |
| Lease acquire | < 50ms from request to grant | gRPC round-trip |
| Agent connect | < 50ms from TCP to session-ready | gRPC handshake |
| Headless CI | Passes on mesa llvmpipe (Linux) | CI pipeline |
| Resource cleanup | Zero residual after lease revoke + disconnect | Telemetry verification |
| Telemetry completeness | Every frame has a valid telemetry record | JSON schema validation |

## Structure

```
crates/
  tze_hud_scene/      # Pure scene graph (Layer 1)
  tze_hud_compositor/  # wgpu compositor (Layer 2)
  tze_hud_runtime/     # Frame pipeline, admission, budgets (Layers 2-3)
  tze_hud_protocol/    # gRPC service definitions + protobuf (Layer 3-4)
  tze_hud_input/       # Input pipeline + hit-region (Layer 5)
  tze_hud_telemetry/   # Structured telemetry (Layer 6)
examples/
  vertical_slice/      # The reference binary that wires everything together
tests/
  vertical_slice/      # Integration tests for the slice
```

## What This Is NOT

- Not a full implementation. This is the thinnest possible path through every layer.
- Not production-ready. Error handling can be minimal. Config can be hardcoded.
- Not feature-complete. One tab, two tiles, one agent, one hit-region. That's enough to prove the architecture.
- The purpose is to find friction: awkward APIs, hidden coupling, performance cliffs, protocol mismatches. If those emerge in the slice, they'd be 10x worse in the full implementation.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `cargo test -p vertical_slice --features headless` passes all assertions
2. `cargo run --example vertical_slice` opens a window with two rendered tiles
3. A test gRPC agent connects, acquires a lease, creates content, receives input events
4. Hit-region responds to pointer input with local feedback < 4ms
5. Structured telemetry JSON is emitted and all metrics meet budgets
6. Developer visibility artifacts are generated (rendered PNG + telemetry JSON)
7. Headless mode works on mesa llvmpipe without a display server
8. All error responses follow the structured error model (code + message + context + hint)
title: Build reference vertical slice: headless scene → window → gRPC agent → lease → hit-region → telemetry

description:
Deep-dive review: compare the original heart-and-soul/ doctrine against the RFCs and vertical slice delivered by sibling beads under this epic.

## Workflow

1. Re-read the epic description and all sibling bead descriptions/acceptance criteria.
2. Re-read all heart-and-soul/ doctrine files (vision.md, architecture.md, presence.md, security.md, privacy.md, failure.md, mobile.md, validation.md, development.md, v1.md, and the new attention.md).
3. Audit each RFC for:
   - Complete coverage of its cited doctrine sections
   - Quantitative requirements with units and percentiles (not aspirations)
   - Explicit protocol/wire format specifications
   - Platform target specificity (Linux/Windows/macOS)
   - Excalidraw diagrams for key concepts
4. Audit the vertical slice for:
   - Every layer exercised (scene graph, compositor, gRPC, lease, input, telemetry)
   - All quantitative success criteria met with telemetry evidence
   - Clean API ergonomics (if the test is awkward, the API is wrong)
5. Produce a checklist mapping every v1.md requirement to its covering RFC section and vertical slice test.
6. For any doctrine requirement NOT covered (or only partially covered):
   a. Create a new child bead under this epic describing the missing work.
   b. Set appropriate priority and link dependencies.
7. If gap beads were created in step 6, create a follow-up reconciliation bead (gen-2) that depends on ALL the new gap beads. Gen-2 re-runs this same workflow. Gen-2 may spawn gen-3 (hard limit).
8. Keep this reconciliation bead open until all gap beads and follow-up reconciliation beads are closed.
9. Close with a coverage summary recording the final requirement-to-RFC mapping.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Every requirement in v1.md has a corresponding RFC section or vertical slice test
2. Every heart-and-soul/ principle cited in the RFCs has quantitative requirements
3. Any gaps found result in new child beads created under the same epic
4. If gaps were found, a follow-up reconciliation bead (gen-2) was created
5. Reconciliation summary is recorded in the close reason
title: Reconcile spec-to-code coverage for tze_hud contract RFCs (gen-1)
